title: Candied walnuts
subtitle: Walnuts coated in caramelised sugar and salt
excerpt: Walnuts coated in a thin, crisp caramel shell and scattered with sea salt flakes. Made in a frying pan in under 10 minutes. Good with cheese, on salads, or as a sweet to eat out of hand. Makes three hundred grams.
sourceNotes: Candied nuts are documented in British and European confectionery from the eighteenth century onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: sugared walnuts made by coating walnut halves in boiled sugar syrup are recorded in the confectionery chapter. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: caramelised nuts are documented as a domestic confection made by heating nuts with sugar in a pan until the sugar coats and caramelises.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a sealed jar at room temperature for up to 2 weeks. Keep dry.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: the sugar should caramelise to an amber around the walnuts; watch closely and remove before it darkens to mahogany

content.text: The sugar goes through a grainy, crystallised stage before it melts back into caramel; do not stop stirring during this phase or some nuts will burn while others are still coated in white sugar. Once the caramel is smooth and amber, move quickly: the nuts need to be separated on the paper before the caramel sets. If hot sugar touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Line a baking tray with lightly oiled baking paper. Place the {{caster-sugar}} and {{walnuts}} in a medium, heavy pan over medium heat. Stir continuously with a wooden spoon. The sugar will first clump, then turn white and grainy as it coats the walnuts, then gradually melt and caramelise around them to amber; about 7–8 minutes in total. As soon as the caramel is smooth and smells nutty rather than burnt, tip the nuts onto the prepared paper and immediately use two forks to separate them into individual pieces or clusters. Scatter the {{sea-salt-flakes}} over the top. Leave to cool and harden for at least 15 minutes before eating.
